response:
Mode: create. Readiness: Stage 2 (model-grounded); the strategic model is supplied, but its behavioral and payoff assumptions are not empirically validated. Eligibility: eligible for the common-policy framing; all six gates pass.

Business problem: A digital service chooses one access-friction level before privately informed users decide whether to participate. Benign and malicious types have different modeled payoffs and respond strategically, but the service cannot observe type or personalize the policy by it. The service asks which common friction level maximizes platform payoff from benign participation net of malicious harm. The structural tension is that stronger friction can deter malicious participation while also discouraging benign users; it is not a universally improving safety lever.

DFC-12: Actor—the service policy owner. Trigger—setting the access rule before participation. Choices—one common friction level. Decision-time information—the type distribution and modeled payoff functions; each user's type and participation choice remain unknown. Stakes—platform payoff from benign participation net of malicious harm. Frictions—the same policy applies to all users. Mechanism—friction changes participation incentives differently across latent types. Trade-off—deterrence versus benign participation. Counterfactual—another feasible common friction level. Mapping—friction is the decision; private type is latent uncertainty; participation is a strategic downstream state/outcome; the common-policy restriction is the constraint; platform payoff is the objective. Evidence—different type sensitivities and strategic response are model assumptions. Boundaries—the model does not identify users before access or establish an absolute safety improvement. Gates: actor pass; timing pass; information pass because the rule does not condition on type; behavior pass under the supplied strategic-response mechanism; constraints pass; objective pass.

Evidence and scholarly limits: Empirical use would require estimates of the type distribution, type-specific payoffs, participation responses, and malicious harm. No supplied evidence supports prevalence, causal deterrence, or broader welfare claims. Scholarly positioning is provisional without a literature comparison or stated analytical contribution. Primary remaining weakness: the latent-type response model is unvalidated.
